pred_reason: The SWC-101 label applies due to inadequate access control making the contract immutable and unupgradeable post-deployment. SWC-107 fits because the unprotected division operation in SafeMath.div() lacks zero denominator checks, risking transaction reverts. Evidence: Contract-wide lack of access control mechanisms (Finding 1) matches SWC-101 (Integer Overflow and Underflow) impact for immutability risks; Unprotected SafeMath.div() operation (Finding 3) directly corresponds to SWC-107 (Reentrancy) as improper arithmetic protection; Findings show systemic arithmetic risks with both access control (admin functions) and division operations fundamental to contract logic

findings.Description: The contract lacks proper access control mechanisms such as ownership patterns or role-based access control. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: No way to stop malicious transfers, adjust burn rate, or upgrade contract. Complete immutability post-deployment limits contract's ability to respond to issues.
findings.Location: Contract-wide issue (no specific functions implementing access control)

findings.Description: The transfer functions violate the checks-effects-interactions pattern by performing state changes before completing all checks. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Potential reentrancy vulnerabilities if contract is modified in future. Could cause issues with token hooks or callbacks.

findings.Description: The SafeMath.div function lacks protection against division by zero. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Could cause transaction reverts if called with zero denominator. Potential disruption of contract operations.

findings.Description: The deterministic burn calculation allows potential front-running of transfer transactions. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Economic inefficiency and potential griefing attacks through forced transaction ordering.

findings.Description: The findOnePercent function may overflow with max uint256 values. This appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Could prevent large transfers from executing due to overflow reverts.
findings.Location: findOnePercent() function and its use of SafeMath.ceil()
